behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: A pure mock of filesystem events won't catch missing recursive directory traversal. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking always returns what you tell it to — a mock won't expose the actual watching logic skipping nested directories. REAL-INFRA: Requires real filesystem structure (nested directories) and actual file placement to verify the watcher recurses properly.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary directory structure with subdirectories, place a test file in a nested subdirectory, trigger the daemon, and verify the file is picked up and processed. Assert that a file at depth>0 produces the same outcome as a file at the root level.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Deduplication is internal state logic — a mock can accurately simulate two consecutive file arrival events. LLM-DEP: No LLM involved. MOCK-HIDE: A mock can be configured to emit the same event twice; the daemon's deduplication logic either suppresses the second or doesn't. Timing can be controlled in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the file arrival event to fire twice for the same file path within the time window (e.g. 100ms). Verify that the transformation pipeline is invoked exactly once. Use a spy to count pipeline invocations.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Mocks emit events in a controlled manner; real filesystems emit multiple events (e.g. 'open', 'write', 'close', or duplicate 'change' events) for a single file operation. MOCK-HIDE: A mock will never expose the real OS behavior that triggers duplicate events. REAL-INFRA: Needs real filesystem write operations to reproduce the duplicate-event scenario.
behaviors.test_description: Write a file to the watched directory using real filesystem operations (fs.writeFile or fs.appendFile), capture all filesystem events emitted by the actual fs.watch or chokidar watcher, and verify the transformation pipeline runs exactly once despite multiple events.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: Requires actual filesystem operations (rmdir, mkdir) and OS watcher recovery behavior. Different OSes (Linux inotify, macOS FSEvents, Windows) handle directory deletion/recreation differently. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking can't expose the actual watcher's inability to re-establish watching on the recreated directory. Requires real subprocess or process state to confirm daemon continues running.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon process. While it's running, delete the watched directory and immediately recreate it. Place a test file in the recreated directory and verify it is processed without restarting the daemon. Check that the daemon logs indicate re-establishment of the watch.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Mocks can be configured to always return events in sorted order, missing real filesystem behavior. MOCK-HIDE: Real filesystems may return events in arbitrary order; mocks won't expose ordering bugs. REAL-INFRA: Requires real filesystem operations with accurate timestamp creation to verify sorting logic works correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files in rapid succession with staggered timestamps in the watched directory. Capture the order in which the daemon picks up files (via logs or output file timestamps) and verify they are processed in timestamp order, not arbitrary order.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Mocks can't expose file-locking or write-in-progress conditions — they return fixed fake file handles. MOCK-HIDE: A mock always returns a 'complete' file state, hiding logic that should check for in-progress writes. REAL-INFRA: Requires real filesystem file operations, file handles, and write-pending state detection (e.g. checking file size growth or using fs.stat to detect modifications).
behaviors.test_description: Open a file for writing in the watched directory without closing it, wait for the arrival event to fire, then continue writing and close. Verify that the file is not processed until the write handle is closed. Confirm the transformation pipeline is invoked only after the file write completes.
